title: Smart Pill Dispenser — STM32-Based Medication Management System

sections.title: Project Background
sections.content: The Smart Pill Dispenser was focused on improving medication management in both hospital and home-care environments. It addresses common medication errors caused by [[forgetfulness]], [[manual scheduling]], and [[human oversight]], especially among elderly users and busy healthcare staff. The system is built using a [[STM32 microcontroller]] and [[automates pill dispensing]] at scheduled times while [[confirming dispensing activity]] through sensors.

sections.title: Problem
sections.content: Many patients, particularly elderly users, forget to take medication or accidentally take it more than once. In hospital settings, [[manual medication handling]] increases [[staff workload]] and raises the risk of [[missed or incorrect doses]] during busy routines.

sections.title: Solution
sections.content: The system [[automates medication dispensing]] by releasing pills at [[predefined times]] and [[verifying the dispensing process]] using sensors. This reduces reliance on memory and manual handling, improving [[medication safety]] and [[consistency]]. In normal mode, the dispenser runs its scheduled cycles automatically, while configuration mode (unlocked via [[RFID]] and [[keypad input]]) is used by authorised staff to adjust or update medication timings before returning to normal operation.

sections.alt: Working principle of the smart pill dispenser
sections.caption: Illustration showing the pill dispensing mechanism, sensor detection, and control flow

sections.alt: System block diagram of the smart pill dispenser
sections.caption: Block diagram showing the STM32 microcontroller, input sensors, user interfaces, and output devices

sections.title: System Flow & Operation
items: System starts in [[normal operating mode]] after power-up.
Authorised staff can enter [[configuration mode]] using [[RFID authentication]].
In configuration mode, the [[keypad]] is used to set or update [[medication schedules]].
Configuration options and system status are shown on the [[OLED display]].
[[RTC]] continuously keeps track of the current time.
When a [[scheduled dispensing time]] is reached, the [[STM32]] triggers the [[dispensing process]].
[[Servo motor]] rotates to release a single pill.
[[IR sensor]] confirms whether a pill has been successfully dispensed.
[[PIR sensor]] detects user presence near the dispenser during operation.
System status, alerts, and feedback are displayed on the [[OLED]] and [[LCD]] screens.
After dispensing, the system returns to [[normal mode]] and waits for the next [[scheduled cycle]].

sections.title: Outcomes
content: Our project was made possible by developing an [[automated pill dispensing system]] using [[STM32 firmware]] and [[Embedded C++]], integrating [[RTC scheduling]], [[servo control]], and [[IR/PIR sensor feedback]] to ensure [[accurate]] and [[reliable dispensing]]. This design reduced [[missed or double doses]] during testing and improved overall [[system safety]] through [[RFID-based configuration access]] that prevented unauthorised changes. The system was validated through repeated [[end-to-end prototype testing]], demonstrating [[stable operation]] across multiple dispensing cycles.

items.alt: Front view of the smart pill dispenser
items.caption: Front view showing keypad and display
Internal dispensing mechanism and servo system
STM32 microcontroller and internal wiring

sections.title: Project Background
sections.content: This project is an [[IoT-based Supermarket Self-Checkout System]] designed to improve [[shopping efficiency]] by allowing customers to independently [[scan items]], [[manage purchases]], and [[complete payments]]. The system supports both [[in-store]] and [[online purchases]], including [[membership authentication]], [[QR code generation]] for in-store pickup, and [[secure payment handling]].

sections.title: Problem
sections.content: A unified self-checkout solution was developed using a [[Raspberry Pi]] as the central controller, integrating [[Picamera-based barcode scanning]], [[RFID authentication]], [[cart management]], and [[payment processing]]. In addition to in-store checkout, the system includes an [[online shopping website]] where users can browse products, add items to a cart, and place orders remotely. Product information, user/cart data, and orders are stored and managed in a [[MariaDB database]]. For online pickup orders, the system generates a [[QR code containing an Order ID]] to enable fast in-store pickup verification. [[Power management logic]] was implemented to reduce energy consumption by dynamically switching between [[low power]] and [[high power operating modes]].

sections.title: Solution
sections.content: A unified self-checkout solution was developed using a Raspberry Pi as the central controller, integrating Picamera-based barcode scanning, RFID authentication, cart management, and payment processing. In addition to in-store checkout, the system includes an online shopping website where users can browse products, add items to a cart, and place orders remotely. Product information, user/cart data, and orders are stored and managed in a MariaDB database. For online pickup orders, the system generates a QR code containing an Order ID to enable fast in-store pickup verification. Power management logic was implemented to reduce energy consumption by dynamically switching between low and high power operating modes.

sections.title: Use Case Diagram
sections.alt: Use case diagram for supermarket self-checkout system
sections.caption: Use case diagram illustrating interactions between in-store customers, online customers, payment gateway, and backend systems

sections.title: Use Case Diagram Explanation
sections.content:
The use case diagram illustrates the functional interactions between different actors and the supermarket self-checkout system. [[In-store customers]] initialize the system, [[scan items]] using a camera, and [[complete payments]]. The scanning workflow includes [[continuous item verification]] against the product database, followed by [[cart total calculation]] and [[checkout confirmation]]. Optional [[membership authentication]] is performed using [[RFID]], extending the payment process with additional validation steps.

[[Online customers]] interact with the system through a website to browse products, add items to the cart, and complete checkout remotely. The website communicates with a [[MariaDB database]] to retrieve product details and store cart/order information. For online pickup purchases, the system generates a [[QR code containing an Order ID]], which is scanned during in-store pickup for verification. The payment gateway supports payment methods such as [[ATM card]], [[PayWave/RFID]], and [[Visa]], while backend services handle [[authentication]], [[item validation]], and [[order summary generation]].

sections.title: Hardware & Interface Architecture
sections.caption: Hardware interface architecture showing sensors, input devices, and output peripherals connected to the Raspberry Pi

sections.title: Hardware Architecture Explanation
sections.content:
The hardware architecture is centered around a [[Raspberry Pi development board]], which serves as the [[main controller]] for all system operations. A [[camera module]] connected via the [[CSI port]] performs [[barcode scanning]] for item identification. An [[RFID reader]] interfaced through [[GPIO]] and [[SPI pins]] enables membership authentication, while a [[keypad]] connected to GPIO pins allows user input for menu navigation and checkout control.

An [[ultrasonic sensor]] connected to GPIO pins is used to detect object presence and system interaction states. Output devices include an [[LCD]] connected through the [[I2C interface]] for displaying menus, item details, and payment information, as well as a [[buzzer]] connected to a GPIO pin to provide audio feedback during authentication and system events. This [[modular hardware design]] ensures reliable [[real-time interaction]] between sensors, user interfaces, and backend software components.

sections.title: System Flow & Operation
items: System powers on and displays the [[main menu]] on the [[LCD interface]]
User initiates checkout and begins [[scanning items]] using the [[camera]]
Scanned items are [[validated against the product database]] and added to the [[cart]]
System continuously updates and displays the [[cumulative total]]
Payment is [[processed securely]] through the [[payment system]]
For online orders, a [[QR code]] is generated and scanned during [[in-store pickup verification]]
System transitions between [[High Power Mode (HPM)]] and [[Low Power Mode (LPM)]] based on activity state

sections.title: Outcomes
sections.content: The system was successfully implemented based on a structured [[requirements specification]] covering [[item scanning]], [[authentication]], [[payment workflows]], [[online ordering]] with [[QR-based pickup verification]], and [[dynamic power management]]. [[Functional testing]] validated [[end-to-end operation]] across in-store and online scenarios, demonstrating [[reliable system behavior]] and effective [[energy-saving transitions]] between Low Power Mode and High Power Mode.

sections.title: Key Functional Features
items: System startup and [[main menu display]] on [[LCD]] with [[keypad navigation]]
In-store item scanning using [[camera-based barcode detection]]
Online shopping support with [[QR code generation]] for in-store pickup verification
[[Low Power Mode (LPM)]]: Activated when the user exits the system or when no high-power activity is detected to [[conserve energy]]
[[High Power Mode (HPM)]]: Activated during scanning, database lookup, cart updates, authentication, payment processing, and QR code generation

sections.title: Technical Summary
items: Power Management: [[Low Power Mode (LPM)]], [[High Power Mode (HPM)]]

title: StockPulse – Stock Trading & Market Analysis Platform
heroAlt: StockPulse stock trading and market analysis platform

sections.title: My Role
items: Designed and implemented the [[frontend UI]] with a focus on [[beginner-friendly financial interactions]]
Developed smooth [[UI animations]] and [[interaction feedback]] to guide user actions
Implemented [[animated sign-out interaction]] for clear session feedback
Improved [[navigation continuity]] using subtle transitions between pages

sections.title: Project Overview
sections.content: StockPulse is a [[web-based stock trading simulation platform]] that allows users to practise [[buying and selling stocks]] using [[virtual funds]]. The platform focuses on [[clarity]], [[accessibility]], and [[visual feedback]] to make stock trading concepts approachable for beginners.

items.caption: Search bar with quick links allowing users to rapidly find companies by name, ticker, or category
Categorized quick links providing fast navigation across different market sectors

sections.title: User-Friendly Navigation Design
sections.content: The [[search and quick links system]] reduces friction for users by providing [[fast access]] to relevant stocks. This design supports [[exploration]] without overwhelming users with excessive data or complex filtering mechanisms.

items.caption: Stock page profile displaying company overview and key metrics
Day’s Range and 52-Week Range sliders visually representing stock price movement
Key metrics including Market Capital, P/E Ratio, Beta, and Dividend Rate presented for quick interpretation
Recommendation trend bars showing analyst sentiment from Strong Buy to Strong Sell
Buy and sell interface with validation feedback to prevent invalid trades

sections.title: Market Data & Interaction Design
sections.content: Stock information is presented using [[visual sliders]], [[color-coded indicators]], and [[recommendation bars]] to simplify interpretation. These elements help users understand [[price movement]], [[valuation]], [[volatility]], and [[market sentiment]] without requiring prior financial expertise.

items.caption: Watchlist allowing users to track selected stocks and monitor price changes
Transaction history displaying past buy and sell actions for review and learning

sections.title: Tracking & Review
sections.content: The [[watchlist]] and [[transaction history]] features support [[reflective learning]] by making trading behavior visible. Users can easily revisit past actions and observe [[trends over time]].

sections.title: Unique Functional Highlights
items: [[Paper trading system]] enabling stock transactions without [[financial risk]]
[[Visual price range sliders]] for intuitive understanding of stock movement
Market metrics including [[Market Capital]], [[P/E Ratio]], [[Beta]], and [[Dividend Rate]]
[[Client-side validation]] preventing invalid trades such as [[insufficient funds]]
[[Watchlist]] and [[transaction history]] for tracking and review

sections.title: Outcome
sections.content: This project resulted in a [[beginner-friendly stock trading simulation platform]] that makes [[complex market data]] easier to understand. By using [[clear visual indicators]], [[interactive UI elements]], and [[subtle animations]], the platform helps users interpret [[stock movements]], [[trading actions]], and [[portfolio performance]] with confidence, even without prior financial knowledge.

title: Self-Navigating Soil Tiller Robot — Arduino-Based Field Prototype
role: Embedded Systems Developer & Tester | Sensor Integration & Control Logic

sections.title: Project Background
sections.content: This project focuses on reducing [[manual agricultural labour]] by developing an [[autonomous soil tilling robot]]. The system navigates a [[predefined path]] while collecting [[soil]] and [[environmental data]], improving [[efficiency]] and reducing [[physical workload]].

sections.title: Problem
sections.content: Traditional soil tilling is [[labour-intensive]], [[time-consuming]], and [[energy-draining]], especially in [[high-temperature environments]]. Manual cultivation requires continuous human effort and contributes to increased [[energy consumption]], making it inefficient and unsustainable for long-term agricultural practices. These challenges highlight the need for [[automation]] to improve efficiency while supporting [[sustainable agricultural development]].

sections.title: Solution
sections.content: To address these challenges, this project introduces an [[autonomous soil tilling robot]] that [[automates navigation]] and [[soil monitoring tasks]]. By integrating [[sensors]], [[control logic]], and [[actuators]], the system reduces reliance on manual labour, [[optimizes energy usage]], and improves [[operational efficiency]]. The solution aligns with [[UN Sustainable Development Goal 9]] by promoting [[innovation]], [[efficiency]], and [[sustainable agricultural technology]].

sections.title: Block Diagram
sections.caption: Block diagram showing the relationship between the Arduino controller, sensors, actuators, and display

sections.title: System Components & Operation
items: The system initializes the [[Arduino controller]], [[sensors]], and [[motors]] upon power-up.
[[IR sensors]] enable autonomous [[line-following navigation]] along a predefined path.
An [[ultrasonic sensor]] continuously monitors for [[obstacles]] and halts movement when required to prevent [[collisions]].
[[Soil moisture]] and [[environmental sensors]] collect [[soil condition]], [[temperature]], and [[humidity data]] during operation.
Based on sensor input, the [[servo motor]] and [[linear actuator]] control mechanical movement and [[watering-related actions]].
Real-time sensor readings and system status are displayed on the [[LCD]] for user feedback and monitoring.

items.title: Soil Moisture Demo
items.caption: Prototype test showing soil moisture measurement and actuator response for watering decision

items.title: Line Following Demo
items.caption: Prototype test demonstrating autonomous line-following navigation and obstacle detection

sections.title: Prototype Behaviour Observation
sections.content: During testing, the robot demonstrated [[stable line-following behaviour]] and [[reliable obstacle detection]]. Sensor readings were updated consistently on the [[LCD]], validating both the [[firmware logic]] and [[hardware integration]]. In addition, [[soil moisture readings]] were used to determine whether watering was required, where the [[linear servo actuator]] was lowered to perform watering when low moisture was detected and remained inactive when sufficient moisture was present.

sections.title: System Design Poster
sections.alt: Soil tiller system poster showing problem, sensors, block diagram, flow chart, and outcomes
sections.caption: Consolidated system poster illustrating the problem statement, sensor integration, control logic, block diagram, implementation steps, outcomes, and future improvements

sections.title: Poster Explanation
sections.content: The poster summarizes the complete [[system design]] in a single view, covering the [[problem statement]], [[sensor roles]], [[actuator control]], [[flow logic]], and [[implementation process]]. It also highlights the [[project outcomes]] and [[proposed future improvements]], making it easier to understand the overall system without referring to separate diagrams.

sections.title: A Glimpse into the Future Farming
Demonstration Context
sections.content: This demonstration reflects the application of [[Industry 4.0 concepts]] in agriculture, where [[sensors]], [[automation]], and [[control logic]] are integrated to enable [[data-driven decision-making]]. By automating [[soil condition assessment]] and [[navigation]], the system reduces reliance on manual labour while supporting [[smarter]] and [[more sustainable farming practices]].

sections.title: Outcomes
content: This project developed an [[autonomous soil tiller]] that automated [[soil monitoring]] and [[navigation]] through integrated [[sensing]] and [[control]]. [[Soil moisture data]] was used to guide [[watering decisions]], and prototype demonstrations validated the application of [[Industry 4.0 concepts]] in agriculture.

title: Trash Segregator — IoT Waste Monitoring & Sorting Prototype
heroAlt: Smart bin introduction slide comparing traditional bin vs smart bin

sections.title: Project Background
sections.content: The Smart Trash Segregator is a concept-driven IoT solution designed to improve waste sorting efficiency in public and residential environments. The project focuses on reducing user confusion and effort during waste disposal by introducing automated sorting, guided feedback, and optional remote monitoring. It supports sustainability goals by encouraging correct recycling behavior and reducing contamination in waste streams.

sections.title: Problem
sections.content: Many users struggle with proper waste separation due to [[unclear bin categories]], [[unpleasant handling]], and [[lack of guidance]], especially in public spaces. Manual sorting often leads to [[incorrect disposal]], reduced [[recycling efficiency]], and increased [[maintenance effort]].

sections.title: User Interviews
sections.content: User interviews were conducted with [[housewives]] and [[members of the public]] to understand current challenges in waste disposal. Housewives highlighted difficulties in determining whether bins were [[full]] due to the absence of [[clear fill-level indicators]], often resulting in [[overflowing trash]]. Public users reported that bins in shared spaces, especially in [[HDB areas]], are frequently full and that waste is often not properly separated. These insights informed the need for [[clearer guidance]], [[automated sorting]], and improved [[bin capacity awareness]] in the system design.

sections.title: Solution
sections.content: The system introduces a [[smart bin]] that automatically detects [[waste characteristics]] and routes items into the [[correct compartment]] using a [[motorized sorting mechanism]]. [[Visual feedback]] is provided through [[indicator LEDs]] to guide users during disposal. Optional [[wireless connectivity]] enables [[remote monitoring]] of bin status, supporting [[maintenance planning]] and [[system scalability]].

items.alt: System flow chart showing waste detection, decision logic, and actuator control
items.caption: Flow chart illustrating the system’s operational logic, including waste detection, classification, and actuator control sequence

items.alt: Block diagram of hardware components and microcontroller connections
items.caption: Block diagram highlighting hardware inputs, microcontroller processing, and output modules such as actuators and indicators

sections.title: System Architecture & Operational Flow
items: The system begins by initializing the [[microcontroller]], [[sensors]], [[indicators]], and [[communication modules]] after power-up.
[[Ultrasonic rangers]] and the [[LDR]] continuously capture input data related to [[waste presence]], [[bin level]], and basic waste characteristics.
Bin identification is determined using [[DIP switch configuration]], allowing the controller to differentiate between [[waste compartments]].
The microcontroller processes all incoming sensor data and executes [[decision logic]] to classify waste and assess bin capacity.
Based on the control decision, a [[servo motor]] actuates the [[mechanical sorting mechanism]] to route waste into the appropriate bin.
[[Laser]] and [[red/green LED indicators]] provide immediate [[visual feedback]] to guide user interaction and indicate system status.
When bin sections reach capacity, the system triggers [[alerts]] and transmits status updates through the [[wireless module]] for monitoring.
The operation cycle completes after feedback and notification are delivered, readying the system for the next interaction.

sections.title: Data Flow Diagram
sections.alt: Data flow diagram showing sensor data transmission and monitoring
sections.caption: Data flow diagram showing how sensor data is processed, transmitted, and visualized through cloud-based monitoring

sections.title: Data Flow Diagram — Data Processing & Monitoring
items: [[Sensor nodes]] continuously collect waste level and status data during operation.
Collected sensor data is transmitted to a [[central controller]] for processing.
The controller [[analyses bin status]] and determines whether [[alerts]] or updates are required.
Relevant data is sent to [[cloud storage]] for [[logging]] and [[analysis]].
A [[dashboard interface]] visualizes system status for monitoring and maintenance.

sections.title: Mobile Monitoring (MIT App Inventor)
sections.content: A lightweight [[mobile monitoring interface]] was developed using [[MIT App Inventor]] to display the bin’s [[fill level]] received from the system’s [[wireless module]]. This enables quick [[remote checking]] of bin status without physically opening the bin, supporting faster [[response]] for clearing and maintenance.

items.alt: Hand-drawn system diagram illustrating smart bin sensing and sorting mechanism
items.caption: Conceptual system diagram illustrating the smart bin architecture, including ultrasonic rangers for trash-level detection, a light sensor and laser for material identification, and a servo motor controlling waste routing

items.alt: Smart bin hardware prototype with sensors and dual compartments
items.caption: Physical prototype setup showing the smart bin with dual compartments, ultrasonic sensors mounted for trash-level detection, and the microcontroller circuitry handling real-time sensing and classification

items.alt: ThingSpeak dashboard displaying trash level data
items.caption: Live cloud dashboard (ThingSpeak) visualising trash-level data and system status, demonstrating real-time data transmission from the smart bin

items.alt: Mobile app displaying trash level notification
items.caption: Mobile application interface using MIT App Inventor showing real-time trash-level notifications, indicating bin capacity status

sections.title: My Role
items: Contributed to [[concept ideation]] and [[feature prioritization]] using feasibility and impact analysis.
Helped design the [[user interaction flow]] based on interview findings and personas.
Assisted in creating [[system diagrams]] including block diagrams and flowcharts.
Supported [[documentation]] and [[presentation]] of the project for evaluation.

sections.title: Technical Summary
items: Connectivity: [[Wireless module]] for monitoring and diagnostics
Mobile Application: [[MIT App Inventor]] for real-time bin level display and status monitoring

title: SP Pals — Social Media App for Singapore Polytechnic Students
role: Mobile UI Developer, UX Flow & System Design
heroAlt: SP Pals app UI mockups showing home feed, search, profiles, chat and messaging

sections.title: At a Glance
sections.content: SP Pals is a [[student-focused social platform]] concept designed for [[Singapore Polytechnic]]. It centralises [[campus updates]] and [[student interaction features]] such as feeds, profiles, search, and messaging, supporting a more [[connected campus community]] experience.

sections.title: Problem
sections.content: Students rely on [[scattered sources]] (multiple apps and groups) to keep up with campus news and peer communication. This [[fragmentation]] makes it harder to [[discover updates]], [[find communities]], and [[interact efficiently]] in one place.

sections.title: Proposed Solution
sections.content: A single [[SP-centric social app]] that combines [[campus updates]] and [[student interaction]]. The design prioritises [[simple navigation]], [[clear UI hierarchy]], and [[familiar social patterns]] so students can adopt it quickly.

items.alt: Overall application interface showing multiple main screens
items.caption: Overall application view presenting the main screens and interface layout

items.alt: Home, Profile, Settings and QR/Share screens
items.caption: Main navigation flow: Home feed → Profile → Settings, with QR / Share for quick profile linking

items.alt: Search, profile viewing, chat list and message screens

Step 2 — Communication flow: Opening chat conversations and sending real-time messages

items.alt: Notifications, post creation and filter options
items.caption: Engagement flow: Notifications and post creation with filters to organise content discovery

sections.title: Core Features
items: [[Home feed]] for campus-related posts and student updates
[[Search functionality]] to quickly find students and relevant content
[[User profiles]] to showcase student identity, activities, and posts
[[Integrated chat box]] for [[real-time messaging]], powered by [[Firebase Realtime Database]]
[[Post filtering options]] to categorize content into [[CCA]], [[Calendar]], and [[Lost & Found]] sections
[[Notification system]] to keep users informed of activity and updates
[[Post creation flow]] for sharing announcements and updates

sections.title: UI/UX Design Decisions
sections.content: The interface follows [[familiar social app patterns]] to reduce learning time. Key flows ([[home → search → profile → chat]]) were mapped to [[minimise taps]], and screens were designed with [[consistent layout]] and [[spacing]] to keep interaction predictable.

sections.title: Unique Functional Highlights
items: [[QR / share profile feature]] to connect quickly without typing usernames
[[Structured user flows]] for search → profile → chat to reduce friction
[[Post filters]] to support browsing and content organisation
[[Campus-focused positioning]] to differentiate from general social platforms

sections.title: My Role
sections.content: I focused on the [[UI/UX flow]], [[Splash Screen]] and [[screen design planning]], including [[navigation structure]], [[interaction flow]] between main modules, and preparing [[polished mockups]] for evaluation.

sections.title: Outcome
sections.content: Designed a [[unified campus social app concept]] by consolidating [[feeds]], [[search]], [[profiles]], and [[real-time messaging]] into a single platform, resulting in a clearer [[end-to-end user experience]] that reduces [[information fragmentation]] and improves [[student engagement]] within the campus community.

sections.title: Recognition
sections.content: This project was [[selected]] and [[featured]] under the [[SP Batey Hackathon & Challenge 2024]].

title: 5G AIoT Face Recognition Application — Smart Access Control
role: AIoT Systems Developer | Computer Vision & Network Integration
heroAlt: 5G AIoT Face Recognition Access Control System

sections.title: Project Overview
sections.content: This project explores how 5G connectivity, Edge Computing (MEC), and Artificial Intelligence can be integrated to create a secure smart access control solution. A Raspberry Pi equipped with a Pi Camera captures live video and transmits it over a 5G Standalone (SA) network to a MEC laptop where facial recognition is performed in real time using CodeProject.AI. The system demonstrates low-latency video transmission, AI-powered identity verification, and automated access control for smart building environments.

sections.title: Problem Statement
sections.content: Traditional access control systems rely on physical keys, access cards, or PIN codes that can be lost, stolen, duplicated, or shared with unauthorized individuals. These methods also require physical interaction and often lack intelligent identity verification. The project addresses these limitations by developing a contactless facial recognition system that leverages 5G communication and edge intelligence to provide secure, real-time authentication.

sections.caption: Overall 5G Facial Recognition System Project Proposal

sections.title: System Architecture
sections.content: The system consists of a Raspberry Pi transmitter equipped with a Pi Camera and RM502Q-AE 5G HAT module, a 5G network infrastructure, and a MEC laptop acting as the receiver and AI processing platform. Live video is streamed through RTSP over the 5G network, allowing the MEC device to perform face detection and recognition with minimal latency while maintaining high video quality.

sections.title: 5G Connectivity
sections.content: To establish end-to-end 5G communication, RM502Q-AE 5G HAT modules were configured on both the transmitter and receiver sides. The Raspberry Pi transmitter streamed data through the 5G network while the MEC laptop accessed the network through a USB-connected 5G HAT. MBIM mode was selected for improved stability and PySerial scripts were developed to automate modem initialization, network registration, public IP acquisition, and connectivity verification.

sections.title: Video Streaming Over 5G
sections.content: Live video captured by the Raspberry Pi Camera was encoded using H.264 through FFmpeg and published through a MediaMTX RTSP server. The receiver connected to the RTSP stream over the 5G network and continuously displayed incoming video frames. Frame skipping and timestamp overlays were implemented to reduce processing overhead while ensuring the displayed feed remained live and synchronized.

sections.title: Face Recognition & Access Control
sections.content: Facial recognition was implemented using CodeProject.AI. Authorized users were registered into the facial recognition database before deployment. During operation, video frames were processed using AI-based face detection and recognition APIs. Detected faces were compared against registered identities, and confidence thresholds were applied to ensure reliable authentication. Recognition results were linked to LED actuation logic, where authorized users triggered an access indicator while unrecognized individuals were denied access.

sections.title: Performance Optimization
sections.content: The initial implementation used a single-threaded architecture, causing the video display to freeze while waiting for AI processing responses. To improve responsiveness, the system was redesigned using a multi-threaded non-blocking architecture. Video rendering and AI processing were separated into independent workflows, frame resizing and JPEG compression were optimized, persistent HTTP connection pooling was introduced, and disk I/O operations were eliminated. These improvements increased performance from approximately 3–5 FPS to over 30 FPS while maintaining smooth video playback and significantly reducing latency.

sections.title: Multiple Face Detection & Activity Logging
sections.content: The system was further enhanced to support multiple-face detection, allowing several individuals to be recognized simultaneously within a single frame. Each detected face was bounded and labeled with its corresponding identity and confidence score. An automated Excel-based logging system was developed to create a real-time audit trail containing timestamps, recognized usernames, and confidence values. Background-thread logging with cooldown mechanisms prevented duplicate entries while maintaining system performance.

sections.title: Project Demonstration
sections.content: The following demonstration showcases the complete end-to-end workflow of the system, including 5G connectivity establishment, RTSP video streaming, AI-powered facial recognition, LED-based access control, multiple-face detection, and automated activity logging.

sections.title: 5G AIoT Facial Recognition Door Access System Demonstration
My Contributions
items: Configured 5G SA connectivity using RM502Q-AE 5G HAT modules.
Developed Python scripts for modem control using PySerial.
Implemented RTSP video streaming using FFmpeg and MediaMTX.
Optimized video processing performance and reduced latency.
Tested end-to-end communication across Raspberry Pi, 5G network, and MEC platforms.

sections.title: Key Achievements
items: Implemented real-time AI facial recognition over a 5G network.
Integrated edge computing through MEC deployment.
Improved frame rate from approximately 3–5 FPS to over 30 FPS.
Demonstrated practical AIoT deployment for smart building security.

title: IoT Secured School Locker — Multi-Factor Authentication System
role: Embedded C++ Firmware Developer | End-to-End Hardware Integration

sections.title: Project Background
sections.content: The IoT Secured School Locker was developed to improve the security of personal storage systems in educational environments. Traditional lockers rely on physical keys or simple PIN codes, which can be lost, duplicated, or shared. This project introduces a multi-factor authentication system that combines RFID verification, OTP-based PIN authentication, wireless notifications, and centralized activity logging to provide enhanced locker security.

sections.title: Problem
sections.content: Conventional school lockers are vulnerable to unauthorized access due to lost keys, shared credentials, and weak authentication methods. In addition, users and administrators have limited visibility into locker access activities, making it difficult to detect suspicious behavior or maintain security records.

sections.title: Solution
sections.content: A secure IoT locker system was developed using a dual-layer authentication mechanism. Users must first authenticate using a registered RFID card before entering a One-Time Password (OTP) through a keypad. The system validates both authentication stages before unlocking the locker. Real-time Telegram notifications are sent whenever access events occur, while centralized activity logs maintain a complete history of locker usage for monitoring and auditing.

sections.title: My Role
items: Developed embedded C++ firmware for locker control logic.
Integrated RFID authentication and OTP verification workflows.
Integrated keypad, RFID reader, and locking mechanisms.
Performed firmware debugging and hardware troubleshooting.

sections.title: System Flow & Operation
items: System initializes RFID reader, keypad, ESP8266, and lock controller.
User scans a registered RFID card.
Upon successful verification, an OTP challenge is generated.
User enters the OTP using the keypad.
Locker unlocks if both authentication stages succeed.
Telegram notification is sent to authorized users.
Access event is recorded in centralized logs.
Locker automatically relocks after access is completed.

sections.title: Outcomes
sections.content: The project successfully demonstrated a secure multi-factor authentication solution for smart locker applications. By combining RFID verification, OTP authentication, wireless alerts, and centralized logging, the system significantly improved access security while providing complete visibility into locker usage activities.

sections.title: Technical Summary
items: Application Domain: Smart Security & Access Control

title: Smart Safety Vest — IoT-Based Construction Worker Monitoring System
role: IoT Developer | Smart City & Design Project
heroAlt: IoT Smart Safety Vest for Construction Workers

sections.title: Project Background
sections.content: The Smart Safety Vest was designed to improve occupational safety for construction workers operating in physically demanding and potentially hazardous environments. Construction personnel are frequently exposed to heat stress, excessive noise levels, poor visibility conditions, and workplace accidents. This project combines IoT sensors, real-time monitoring, automated alerts, and cloud analytics to provide proactive worker safety management.

sections.title: Problem
sections.content: Construction workers face various occupational risks including heat exhaustion, excessive noise exposure, falls, and environmental hazards. Traditional safety equipment provides passive protection but lacks the ability to continuously monitor worker conditions or generate real-time alerts when dangerous situations occur.

sections.title: Solution
sections.content: A smart safety vest was developed using multiple environmental and motion sensors to continuously monitor worker conditions. Temperature sensors track heat stress, acoustic sensors measure noise exposure, accelerometers detect falls or sudden impacts, and light sensors monitor environmental visibility. Sensor data is transmitted to a cloud dashboard where automated analysis triggers email alerts and activates warning devices such as LEDs and cooling mechanisms when safety thresholds are exceeded.

sections.title: System Flow & Operation
items: Sensors continuously collect worker and environmental data.
Acoustic sensor measures surrounding noise levels.
Accelerometer detects falls and abnormal movements.
Sensor data is transmitted to the cloud platform.
Email notifications are automatically triggered when risks are detected.
Cooling mechanisms are activated during heat stress conditions.

sections.title: Outcomes
sections.content: The project successfully demonstrated how IoT technologies can enhance worker safety through real-time monitoring and automated intervention. The smart vest provided continuous environmental awareness, rapid incident detection, and proactive alerting mechanisms, supporting safer working conditions in construction and industrial environments.

sections.title: Technical Summary
items: Actuators: LED Indicators, Stepper Motor Cooling System
Application Domain: Worker Safety & Smart City Solutions
